tossups.question: Upsidedown urban landscapes were shown using this technique in Marja Parila’s series In Strindberg’s Rooms. Shimon Attie used this process to display images of pre-Holocaust Jews in contemporary Berlin’s Jewish quarter in the piece Writing on the Wall. Polish American artist Krzysztof Wodiczko is best known for installing guerilla art pieces in public spaces using this technique, such as in his series In the Border. Creative use of this technique is the most notable feature of the annual Utah Halloween attraction (*) Bates Haunt. Her trademark papercut silhouettes and color added by this process make up Kara Walker’s Darkytown Rebellion. Before being later published in a book, the original photos in Nan Goldin’s The Ballad of Sexual Dependency were originally displayed using this technique. A series of works by Jenny Holzer beginning with the word “for” used this technique to display ironic quotes on government buildings. For 10 points, what technique allows artists to superimpose large temporary text on public walls?
tossups.question_sanitized: Upsidedown urban landscapes were shown using this technique in Marja Parila's series In Strindberg's Rooms. Shimon Attie used this process to display images of pre-Holocaust Jews in contemporary Berlin's Jewish quarter in the piece Writing on the Wall. Polish American artist Krzysztof Wodiczko is best known for installing guerilla art pieces in public spaces using this technique, such as in his series In the Border. Creative use of this technique is the most notable feature of the annual Utah Halloween attraction (*) Bates Haunt. Her trademark papercut silhouettes and color added by this process make up Kara Walker's Darkytown Rebellion. Before being later published in a book, the original photos in Nan Goldin's The Ballad of Sexual Dependency were originally displayed using this technique. A series of works by Jenny Holzer beginning with the word "for" used this technique to display ironic quotes on government buildings. For 10 points, what technique allows artists to superimpose large temporary text on public walls?
tossups.answer: projection [or using a slide show; accept using a projector; accept projection mapping]
tossups.answer_sanitized: projection [or using a slide show; accept using a projector; accept projection mapping]

tossups.question: Members of this family made a series of tontines, or “mortality lotteries,” with fellow “Musketeer” Bill Frohlich. In the 1950s, a member of this family bribed the official Henry Welch while pioneering a form of direct marketing at the advertising firm William Douglas McAdams. A secretary with the pseudonym “Ann Hedonia” scoured internet forums for this family, as disclosed in a 2021 book by Patrick Radden Keefe. Laura Poitras’s documentary (*) All the Beauty and the Bloodshed recounts protests against this family by Nan Goldin’s P.A.I.N. Group. This family, who names an Asian art gallery in DC that’s adjacent to the Freer Gallery, paid $6 billion in a 2022 settlement that reorganized their company into the public-benefit corporation Knoa. For 10 points, a wing at the Met was formerly named after what family profited off the spread of OxyContin as owners of Purdue Pharma?
tossups.answer: Sackler family [accept specific family members such as Arthur Sackler] (Patrick Radden Keefe’s book is Empire of Pain.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Sackler family [accept specific family members such as Arthur Sackler] (Patrick Radden Keefe's book is Empire of Pain.)

This family, who names an Asian art gallery in DC that's adjacent to the Freer Gallery, paid $6 billion in a 2022 settlement that reorganized their company into the public-benefit corporation Knoa. For 10 points, a wing at the Met was formerly named after what family profited off the spread of OxyContin as owners of Purdue Pharma?

tossups.question: The bombastic “Jane Castleton” works at one of these places in a video by Andrea Fraser titled for its “Highlights.” The death of PKK fighter Andrea Wolf inspired a 2013 video analogizing these places to battlefields, created by Hito Steyerl. Marcel Broodthaers (“bro-TAIRS”) established one of these places in his Brussels home. Two Plexiglas boxes with automatic counters were used in a 1970 work in which people in one of these places were polled on their views about Nelson Rockefeller. Hans Haacke helped pioneer an inquiry into these places called institutional critique. Nan Goldin’s PAIN group protested the Sackler family’s support of one of these places by occupying the ascending spiral ramp of its all-white rotunda. For 10 points, name these buildings that include the Tate Modern and the MoMA.
tossups.answer: art museums [or art gallery or art galleries; prompt on art exhibits or art exhibitions] (The penultimate line refers to the Guggenheim.)

packet.name: D – Berkeley A, South Carolina A, UVA B

tossups.answer_sanitized: art museums [or art gallery or art galleries; prompt on art exhibits or art exhibitions] (The penultimate line refers to the Guggenheim.)

tossups.question: Glenn Ligon responded to an exhibition from this decade with a piece that places critical placards between photographs of Black men from that exhibition. In a photograph taken in this decade, the artist lies in bed staring at her cigarette-smoking lover, whose abusive behavior was later depicted in a photograph that shows the artist “one month after being battered.” The Ballad of Sexual Dependency, a slideshow exhibition by (*) Nan Goldin, debuted in this decade. Calla lilies, orchids, and other flowers appear in the XYZ Portfolios of a piece from this decade, which also included black-and-white photographs of homoerotic sadomasochism. For 10 points, The Perfect Moment was first exhibited during what decade, in which photographer Robert Mapplethorpe died of AIDS?
tossups.answer: 1980s (The piece in the first line is Notes on the Margin of the Black Book.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: 1980s (The piece in the first line is Notes on the Margin of the Black Book.)

tossups.question: A close-up photo of two of these performers in a taxi was taken by Nan Goldin, who began her career capturing these performers. Labels in the shape of black cat heads were distributed to these performers to protect them from arrest by Jose Sarria, who founded the Imperial Court System. Jean-Michel brings his in-laws to Saint-Tropez, an establishment employing several of these performers, in the Harvey Fierstein musical La Cage aux Follies. A charity organization of these performers modeled after an order of nuns, known as the Sisters of Perpetual (*) Indulgence, was established in 1979 in the Castro district. Precursors to these performers include travesti roles in theater, such as pantomime dames and trouser roles. These performers lob insulting observations in a practice called “reading” and may engage in “tucking” by hiding parts of their body under their costumes. For 10 points, name these performance artists who dress in defiance of expected gender roles, and are classified into “kings” and “queens.”
tossups.answer: drag artists [or drag kings; or drag queens; accept female impersonators; reluctantly prompt on cross-dressers; prompt on transvestites]
tossups.answer_sanitized: drag artists [or drag kings; or drag queens; accept female impersonators; reluctantly prompt on cross-dressers; prompt on transvestites]

tossups.question: A song from this musical work names a slideshow of photos depicting drag queens, heroin use, and other aspects of post-Stonewall Bowery culture by Nan Goldin. A song in this work that obsessively returns to a C major sixth chord was written at the demand of Harald Paulsen. Nina Simone covered a revenge song from this work sung from the perspective of a maid who imagines pirates leveling her town. “The Ballad of Sexual Dependency” and “What Keeps (*) Mankind Alive?” appear in this work's second act, during which the prostitute Jenny sends a character to jail despite the efforts of police chief Tiger Brown. One song contrasts a shark's teeth with a murderer's jackknife and was covered by Bobby Darin. Polly Peachum marries Macheath in this work's first act. “Mack the Knife” opens, for 10 points, what adaptation of The Beggar's Opera by Kurt Weill and Bertolt Brecht?
tossups.question_sanitized: A song from this musical work names a slideshow of photos depicting drag queens, heroin use, and other aspects of post-Stonewall Bowery culture by Nan Goldin. A song in this work that obsessively returns to a C major sixth chord was written at the demand of Harald Paulsen. Nina Simone covered a revenge song from this work sung from the perspective of a maid who imagines pirates leveling her town. "The Ballad of Sexual Dependency" and "What Keeps (*) Mankind Alive?" appear in this work's second act, during which the prostitute Jenny sends a character to jail despite the efforts of police chief Tiger Brown. One song contrasts a shark's teeth with a murderer's jackknife and was covered by Bobby Darin. Polly Peachum marries Macheath in this work's first act. "Mack the Knife" opens, for 10 points, what adaptation of The Beggar's Opera by Kurt Weill and Bertolt Brecht?

bonuses.leadin: Under this man's direction, Pfizer expanded its sales team from 8 to 2000 and his sales techniques led to Valium becoming the first drug to reach $100 million. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this man who has a namesake gallery at the Smithsonian for his donation of 1000 pieces of Asian art.
Photographer Nan Goldin's attempts to get the Sackler name removed from museums was chronicled in this film, nominated for Best Documentary Feature at the 2022 Academy Awards.

The backlash against the Sackler family was caused by their contribution to this crisis. Their marketing of Oxycotin through their company Purdue Pharma led to a $5.5 billion settlement for their contribution to this crisis.
answers_sanitized: All the Beauty and the Bloodshed

bonuses.leadin: Answer some questions about the French conceptual photographer Sophie Calle (kal), for 10 points each:
parts: Calle’s film No Sex Last Night ends at a chapel in this city. Denise Scott Brown’s photos appear in a book titled for this city that compares the “decorated shed” to the “duck.”

In a suite named for this city, Calle described following a man named Henri B. Nan Goldin’s druggy film Sirens appeared at this Italian city’s 2022 biennale (byen-NAH-le), which is held in its Arsenale shipyard.

Calle included matter-of-fact reports of these places in one series. A Yves Klein manifesto titled for one of them states that “birds must be eliminated,” and Andreas Gursky depicted one in his photo Shanghai.
answers: Venice [accept Suite Vénitienne; accept Venetian Suite; accept Venice Biennale; accept La Biennale di Venezia]
hotels [or hotel rooms; prompt on rooms; accept Chelsea Hotel manifesto]
answers_sanitized: Venice [accept Suite Venitienne; accept Venetian Suite; accept Venice Biennale; accept La Biennale di Venezia]
hotels [or hotel rooms; prompt on rooms; accept Chelsea Hotel manifesto]

bonuses.leadin: On November 7, China sentenced one man to death, two to life imprisonment, and six others to varying sentences for trafficking this drug. For 10 points each:
parts: The abuse of what synthetic opioid pain killer, often used by addicts instead of heroin, has ravaged many communities in the U.S.?
This pharmaceutical company primarily owned by members of the Sackler family has been much blamed for the spread of fentanyl and another of its products, oxycodone.
The photographer Nan Goldin led a group of protestors who unfurled an anti-Sackler-family banner in July, 2019, on the glass pyramid in front of this European museum.
